settlement.rules: If the first new person to be Attorney General is <CANDIDATE> before Jan 20, 2029, then the market resolves to Yes.
settlement.rulesSecondary: Acting and Interim holders of the office are not included in the Payout Criterion. Persons confirmed by the Senate to be that position, or appointed via a “recess appointment” are included, even if that recess appointment is later struck down as illegal.
settlement.actingInterim: excluded — per rules: "Acting and Interim holders of the office are not included in the Payout Criterion. Persons confirmed by the Senate to be that position, or appointed via a “recess appointment” are included, even if that recess appointment is later struck down as illegal."

notes: Top contract: Todd Blanche at 44¢ YES.
Price alone is not a pick — see Sections 4, 7, 8 for context.

facts.claim: The U.S. Senate confirmed Pam Bondi as the 87th Attorney General of the United States by a 54-46 vote.
Pam Bondi was sworn in as Attorney General at the White House by Justice Clarence Thomas, with President Trump in attendance.
President Trump named Todd Blanche as Deputy Attorney General nominee; Blanche was later confirmed and serves as the No. 2 official at DOJ.
Lee Zeldin was confirmed as Administrator of the Environmental Protection Agency, taking him out of any near-term DOJ leadership pipeline.

President Trump announced he was withdrawing Ed Martin's nomination for U.S. Attorney for the District of Columbia after senators signaled he lacked the votes, and instead said he would name Jeanine Pirro as interim U.S. Attorney for D.C.
Harmeet Dhillon was confirmed by the Senate as Assistant Attorney General for the Civil Rights Division at the Department of Justice.

Alina Habba's interim term as U.S. Attorney for New Jersey expired and the Trump administration moved to keep her in the role via reassignment, after federal judges declined to extend her appointment; she remained a subject of ongoing legal disputes over her authority.
Jay Clayton was nominated by President-elect Trump to serve as U.S. Attorney for the Southern District of New York.
Ken Paxton announced he would challenge Senator John Cornyn in the 2026 Texas Republican Senate primary, signaling his political focus is on the Senate race rather than a federal DOJ appointment.
Andrew Bailey was sworn in for a full term as Missouri Attorney General after winning election in November 2024.
Ron DeSantis was re-elected Governor of Florida; his current term runs through January 2027, limiting any near-term federal appointment without resignation.

Attorney General Pam Bondi faced internal criticism from MAGA allies over DOJ's handling of the Jeffrey Epstein files but retained President Trump's public backing, with Trump calling her a 'fantastic' AG.
President Trump publicly reiterated support for Attorney General Bondi amid speculation about Cabinet changes, telling reporters she was doing a 'great job.'
Ed Martin, after his U.S. Attorney nomination was withdrawn, was reassigned within DOJ to lead a 'Weaponization Working Group' and serve as Associate Deputy Attorney General and pardon attorney.

candidates.name: Todd Blanche
strengths: INFERENCE: As current Deputy AG, Blanche has the shortest institutional path to the AG chair - he already runs day-to-day DOJ operations and would be the default succession choice if Bondi departs, minimizing transition friction.

INFERENCE: His prior role as Trump's personal criminal defense lawyer establishes the deepest possible personal loyalty signal, which is the single strongest predictor of Trump AG picks in the second term.

INFERENCE: Already cleared a Senate confirmation vote for DAG in 2025, meaning his FBI background check, financial disclosures, and vote-whipping coalition are pre-built and recent - a re-confirmation for AG faces lower marginal vetting risk than any outside pick.
INFERENCE: No competing executive portfolio to unwind (unlike Zeldin at EPA or DeSantis as governor), so elevation creates no secondary vacancy fight inside the administration.
weaknesses: INFERENCE: Perception as 'Trump's personal lawyer' could activate the same Democratic bloc opposition and one or two GOP institutionalist defections (Collins/Murkowski profile) that nearly sank prior loyalist nominees, narrowing the floor margin.
INFERENCE: His value to Trump may actually be higher as DAG-as-enforcer than as AG-as-public-face, creating an internal logic against promotion even if Bondi exits.
INFERENCE: Lacks the prosecutorial-management or elected-official profile that gives cover to wavering senators looking for a 'qualifications' rationale to vote yes.
processObstacles: INFERENCE: Promotion is conditional on a Bondi vacancy actually opening before Jan 20 2029 - absent that triggering event, Blanche cannot be confirmed as AG regardless of preference.

INFERENCE: Senate floor time for a contested AG re-confirmation competes with judicial nominations and appropriations; a late-term vacancy (2028) could run out of calendar before a confirmed successor is seated, pushing resolution past the market deadline.

candidates.name: Lee Zeldin
strengths: INFERENCE: Already Senate-confirmed once this Congress (as EPA Administrator), demonstrating he can clear the 50-vote threshold with the current GOP coalition intact.

INFERENCE: Law degree plus House Armed Services/Foreign Affairs record gives him a 'qualified former member' frame that eases votes from institutionalist Republicans who balked at less conventional picks.
INFERENCE: Strong MAGA-aligned voting record and 2022 NY gubernatorial run preserved loyalty credentials without the personal-lawyer baggage that constrains Blanche.
weaknesses: INFERENCE: Has no federal prosecutorial or DOJ-management experience, which becomes a harder sell for AG than for EPA and invites 'wrong job' criticism even from friendly senators.

INFERENCE: His confirmed-and-serving status at EPA is a revealed preference by Trump that Zeldin's highest-and-best use is environmental deregulation, not DOJ - lateral moves of confirmed cabinet officers to AG are historically rare.
INFERENCE: Lower prior-probability signal: at 27¢ versus Blanche's 43¢ he is the market's clear backup, not the base case, implying he only wins in scenarios where Blanche is also ruled out.
processObstacles: INFERENCE: Moving Zeldin requires sequencing two confirmations - a new EPA Administrator plus Zeldin-as-AG - doubling Senate floor demand and creating two simultaneous vetting tracks.
INFERENCE: Vacating EPA mid-deregulatory-agenda creates policy-continuity costs that the White House would weigh against the AG benefit, raising the bar for the swap to actually happen.

candidates.name: Jeanine Pirro
strengths: INFERENCE: Genuine prosecutorial background (former Westchester DA) plus current service as interim US Attorney for DC gives her the only resume on this list that combines elected-prosecutor experience with active DOJ line authority.
INFERENCE: Extremely high personal-loyalty and public-defense quotient with Trump from years of Fox programming, placing her in the inner trust circle that drives second-term picks.
weaknesses: INFERENCE: Her interim US Atty DC appointment was itself a workaround that suggested the White House judged a full Senate confirmation difficult - that same vote-count problem scales up, not down, for AG.
INFERENCE: Long record of partisan television commentary gives Democrats a ready-made hostile-witness package and gives moderate Republicans a 'temperament' justification to defect.
INFERENCE: Age and the absence of any prior federal Senate-confirmation makes her a higher-variance pick than candidates with recent successful confirmations on the books.
processObstacles: INFERENCE: Interim US Attorney status is itself process-fragile (court-appointment and 120-day clock dynamics); her bandwidth and political capital may be consumed defending that role rather than ascending.

INFERENCE: A Pirro AG nomination would likely require a near-unanimous GOP whip operation; a single high-profile GOP 'no' (Collins/Murkowski/McConnell-aligned) sinks it and the White House knows this ex ante, depressing the probability the nomination is even sent.

candidates.name: Ron DeSantis
strengths: INFERENCE: Yale/Harvard Law plus Navy JAG plus US Attorney's office experience plus two-term governor of a major state gives him the strongest pure-credentials profile of any candidate on the board.

INFERENCE: As a sitting governor he commands an independent political base, which paradoxically makes Senate Republicans less willing to vote against him for fear of cross-pressure from their own state parties.
weaknesses: INFERENCE: The 2024 primary rivalry created lasting personal friction with Trump that cuts directly against the loyalty heuristic driving every other pick in this market - this is the dominant reason he trades at 2¢.

INFERENCE: DeSantis has shown no public interest in a cabinet subordinate role and has post-governorship presidential ambitions of his own that are better served outside the administration than inside it.
INFERENCE: Accepting AG would require subordinating to a Trump White House on prosecutorial decisions, a posture inconsistent with his established executive-principal brand.
processObstacles: INFERENCE: Florida governor term runs into Jan 2027; mid-term resignation creates a succession event (Lt Gov ascends) with its own Florida political costs that DeSantis would have to absorb.

INFERENCE: Even if offered and accepted, the nomination-confirmation-resignation choreography is unusually heavy and unlikely to be initiated unless a Bondi vacancy aligns precisely with DeSantis's own exit calendar.

candidates.name: Harmeet Dhillon
strengths: INFERENCE: Already Senate-confirmed in 2025 as Assistant AG for Civil Rights, so the confirmation machinery, vetting file, and recent whip count exist - the marginal lift to elevate her is lower than for an outside pick.
INFERENCE: Long-standing MAGA-legal-movement credentials (RNC committeewoman, election-litigation portfolio) lock in the loyalty signal Trump prioritizes.
INFERENCE: Sitting inside DOJ Main Justice already gives her institutional knowledge and a credible 'continuity' frame if Bondi exits abruptly.
weaknesses: INFERENCE: Civil Rights AAG is a substantial jump below AG in the DOJ hierarchy - skipping past the Deputy AG (Blanche) would require an explicit Trump preference reversal that markets have no reason to forecast.
INFERENCE: Her 2025 AAG confirmation was close and drew GOP defections; an AG vote would face the same or worse coalition, with less margin for error than Blanche or Zeldin.
INFERENCE: Public profile as a movement litigator rather than a manager raises 'can she run a 115,000-person department' objections that wavering senators can hide behind.
processObstacles: INFERENCE: Promotion requires both a Bondi vacancy AND Blanche being passed over or also departing - a compound conditional that depresses base-rate probability sharply.
INFERENCE: Elevating Dhillon vacates the Civil Rights AAG slot at a politically sensitive moment, forcing a parallel confirmation fight that competes for Senate floor time inside the same window.

favoriteWrong: Blanche's conflict-of-interest baggage (former personal defense attorney for Trump in the Jack Smith cases) is exactly the profile that drew Republican defections on Gaetz; Sen. Thom Tillis has already publicly signaled skepticism, and Collins/Murkowski/McConnell make a 3-vote loss plausible — with a 53-seat GOP majority that kills confirmation absent a recess maneuver.

Blanche is performing the AG job right now as Acting AG with 'full authority.' That removes Trump's urgency to spend political capital on a bruising confirmation fight when he can simply leave Blanche in place under FVRA / DAG-succession logic. 'Status quo acting' is the single most underpriced outcome and it directly subtracts from Blanche's confirmation probability.

The 43¢ price assumes Trump actually nominates Blanche. Trump has a documented pattern (Gaetz → Bondi) of pulling controversial picks and pivoting to a 'safer' confirmable Republican. If the whip count looks ugly, Blanche gets withdrawn and someone else (Zeldin, a sitting senator, a red-state AG) becomes the NEW confirmed AG — Blanche himself never clears the bar.

Market is anchoring on 'who is running DOJ today' rather than 'who will be the next NEW Senate-confirmed AG.' These are different questions. Acting service does not count under the rules, so every additional month Blanche stays acting actually erodes, not strengthens, his confirmation odds (Senate calendar compression, midterm posture, donor fatigue).

Echo-chamber effect: the X discourse describing Blanche as 'frontrunner' is largely sourced to Trump allies lobbying publicly — that is advocacy, not signal. Real frontrunner pricing for a confirmable nominee should be in the 25–35¢ range, not 43¢.

Base rate check: high-conflict loyalist AG nominees in modern history (Meese, Gonzales, Sessions-recusal aftermath, Gaetz) face confirmation drag or withdrawal. Pricing Blanche above Zeldin by 16 points implies a confirmation-probability gap that the underlying Senate math does not support.
secondUnderpriced: Zeldin is the canonical 'Trump pulls Blanche, pivots to a clean Republican' fallback: already Senate-confirmed once (EPA), no criminal-defense baggage, House Judiciary alum, lawyer, and pre-vetted by the same GOP caucus that would have to confirm him. 27¢ understates the conditional probability given a Blanche withdrawal.

Zeldin is fungible — he can be moved out of EPA without a cabinet-level fight, unlike pulling a sitting senator (which costs a Senate seat) or a sitting governor. That makes him the path of least resistance for a White House that wants a confirmation win.
If the market correctly prices P(Blanche withdrawn or defeated) at 40–55%, and Zeldin captures even 35–45% of that conditional space, fair value is closer to 30–38¢, not 27¢.
Zeldin has been named in multiple independent reporting cycles (not just X allies) as 'being eyed,' which is a stronger signal than Blanche's allies-lobbying-on-X pattern.

Zeldin's downside is capped: even if he is not picked, the same logic (pre-confirmed, low-baggage Republican) makes him a structural hedge against the entire 'Blanche fails' branch, so the 27¢ should carry a liquidity premium it currently lacks.
settlementTraps: No-confirmation tail: Bondi already departed, Blanche serves as Acting AG indefinitely under FVRA + DAG-succession, no permanent nominee ever clears the Senate before Jan 20 2029 → market resolves NO to every named candidate. This 'long acting' branch is materially nonzero and gets zero direct ticker on Kalshi; it has to be inferred from the residual.

Recess appointment ambiguity: rules say recess appointments count 'even if struck down.' But what about an intra-session adjournment-clause appointment that the Senate never recognizes as a recess (NLRB v. Noel Canning territory)? Could resolve YES on a technicality that markets are not modeling, or NO if Kalshi interprets 'recess' strictly.

Tie-vote / VP-breaking: a 50-50 confirmation with VP Vance breaking the tie counts as a normal confirmation, but if a senator is absent / hospitalized / dies, a 49-49 or 50-49 vote could leave the nominee in limbo for weeks. Settlement timing matters if competing nominations are stacked.

Withdrawn-then-resubmitted same person: if Blanche is nominated, withdrawn, then re-nominated and confirmed, he is still the 'first NEW' AG. Markets sometimes mis-handle the identity-vs-process distinction.

Definition of 'NEW': Blanche is currently Acting AG. If Trump uses a recess appointment to install Blanche himself, does Kalshi count that as the 'first NEW AG' or treat Blanche as not-new because he was already running DOJ? Rule text says recess appointments are included, which implies YES on Blanche-via-recess, but the 'NEW person' phrasing is exploitable.

Cross-market contamination: if there are separate Kalshi tickers for 'Blanche confirmed by [date]' vs 'next AG is Blanche,' arbitrage flow can distort this market's prints without reflecting fundamentals.
narrativeTraps: Trump-allies-on-X effect: loud public endorsements of Blanche (JonHollis-type accounts, MAGA influencers) get scraped by prediction-market traders as 'signal' when they are actually a coordinated lobbying campaign aimed at Trump himself — i.e., the noise IS the strategy.
Recency illusion: Blanche is running DOJ today, so traders mentally collapse 'current AG' into 'next confirmed AG.' The market is pricing presence, not Senate math.

Media speculation laundering: cable hits and Politico/Axios 'sources say' pieces recycle the same 2-3 names (Blanche, Zeldin, maybe a sitting senator), creating an illusion of a narrow field when the true distribution includes dark-horse state AGs, federal judges, and sitting US Attorneys.
Gaetz-amnesia: traders who lived through the Gaetz withdrawal in late 2024 are now under-weighting the same dynamic for Blanche despite a near-identical conflict profile.

Survivorship bias in the X timeline: posts predicting Blanche's confirmation get amplified; posts noting Tillis/Collins skepticism get buried. Sentiment-scraping bots feed this asymmetry back into the order book.

False binary framing: discourse treats it as 'Blanche vs Zeldin' when the rule-relevant outcome space includes (a) neither — long acting tenure to 2029, (b) a surprise pick, (c) recess-appointment gambit that may or may not settle YES depending on Kalshi adjudication.

narratives.claim: Todd Blanche is currently Acting Attorney General after Bondi was removed in early April 2026 and is widely viewed as the frontrunner for the permanent role; some accounts say he is 'auditioning' via aggressive DOJ activity.
Blanche recently appeared before the Senate for confirmation to become permanent AG; Jeanine Pirro publicly endorsed him saying he 'will be a great AG.'
Critics highlight Blanche conflict-of-interest concerns due to his prior role as Trump's personal defense lawyer.
Lee Zeldin remains a leading speculated AG pick; renewed buzz on May 21, 2026 after a prominent Oval Office appearance with Trump and a closed-door lunch interpreted as a 'job interview.'
Zeldin himself deflected AG questions ~May 10, saying his 'focus 100%' is being the best EPA Administrator.
Pam Bondi was fired (not resigned) by Trump in early April 2026, reportedly tied to Epstein files handling and a congressional subpoena.
Jeanine Pirro was reportedly 'gunning for the AG job' but was instead appointed US Attorney for DC; supporters still occasionally call for her to get the top role.

Ed Martin was NEVER Trump's AG pick — his DC US Attorney nomination was blocked by Sen. Tillis over Jan 6 / pardon comments; he was redirected to Pardon Attorney and later removed. Speculative AG chatter around him is fringe.
Harmeet Dhillon (currently AAG for Civil Rights) is floated by base conservatives as a strong AG candidate or for promotion to Associate AG; viewed as base-friendly.
Ron DeSantis and Ken Paxton appear only as 'long shot' names in commentator lists of possible AG picks; no reporting of actual consideration.
Conservative shortlists for AG most commonly include Dhillon, Zeldin, Pirro, and Sen. Eric Schmitt; Alina Habba, DeSantis, Paxton mentioned as long shots.
Trump publicly praised Blanche, reportedly saying he 'kept me out of jail for years' — narrative reinforcing Blanche as the loyalty-driven pick.
